modification:
Diagnosis: The agent is defaulting to exactly 50% in ranging/neutral conditions and ignoring the empirical base rate that BTC 5-min candles close UP slightly more than 50% of the time (the market price itself often reflects this at ~50.5%). By anchoring to 50% instead of the market price as the baseline, the agent adds no value and achieves poor Brier scores when the outcome is YES.
Old text: - If no momentum or news, default to 50% with low confidence
New text: - If no momentum or news, default to the current market price as your baseline estimate with low confidence
Expected effect: By using the market price as the neutral baseline instead of a hard 50%, the agent will better capture any small structural bias already priced in, reducing Brier score in no-edge scenarios.

Diagnosis: When the market price is near 50% and there's no momentum, the agent defaults to exactly 50% instead of deferring to the market price. In several worst cases, the market price was 0.495 or 0.505, but the agent rounded to 0.5, ignoring the small signal embedded in the market price itself.
Old text: - If no momentum or news, default to the current market price as your baseline estimate with low confidence
New text: - If no momentum or news, output the current market price exactly as your estimate (do not round to 0.5) with low confidence
Expected effect: The agent will preserve the market's own probability signal rather than discarding small deviations from 50%, reducing unnecessary error when the market has already priced in a slight directional lean.

Diagnosis: The agent is ignoring the market price as a signal, defaulting to 50% even when the market is pricing meaningfully away from 50% (e.g., 61.5%). The market price itself contains aggregated information the agent should incorporate rather than override with a flat base rate.
Old text: - Never deviate more than 10pp from 50% on statistical patterns alone — edge is thin at this timescale
New text: - Never deviate more than 10pp from 50% on statistical patterns alone — edge is thin at this timescale
- Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 5pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 40% own analysis and 60% market price, then report the blended result
Expected effect: By partially deferring to the market price when it diverges significantly from 50%, the agent will avoid large negative edges like the -0.115 case and produce estimates closer to the true probability.

Diagnosis: The agent ignores the market price when it deviates less than 5pp from 50%, even when the market price contains meaningful signal (e.g., 0.615 is a strong directional signal that was completely disregarded). The 5pp threshold is too high, causing the agent to systematically discard actionable market information.
Old text: - Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 5pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 40% own analysis and 60% market price, then report the blended result
New text: - Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 2pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 40% own analysis and 60% market price, then report the blended result
Expected effect: By lowering the threshold from 5pp to 2pp, the agent will incorporate market pricing signal more often, reducing cases where a clearly directional market price (e.g., 0.615) is ignored in favor of a flat 50% estimate.

Diagnosis: The agent over-weights the market price signal when it deviates even slightly from 50%, blending 60% market price into its estimate even when the market price is only 2pp away from 50% (e.g., 0.495 or 0.505). This causes the agent to anchor too heavily on potentially noisy market prices rather than maintaining calibrated independence, and in the worst case (32% prediction, actual YES) the market price dragged the estimate far from the base rate without sufficient justification.
Old text: - Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 2pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 40% own analysis and 60% market price, then report the blended result
New text: - Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 5pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 60% own analysis and 40% market price, then report the blended result. Deviations smaller than 5pp from 50% should be treated as noise and ignored.
Expected effect: By raising the threshold for market price incorporation and reducing its weight, the agent will stay closer to the base rate when market signals are weak, avoiding large errors caused by over-trusting small or noisy market price deviations.

Diagnosis: The agent under-weights the market price when it deviates significantly from 50%, using only a 40% weight on the market signal even when the market is highly informed (e.g., 78.5% or 25.5%). This causes the agent to anchor too strongly on its own weak statistical signals and produce estimates that diverge badly from an informative market consensus.
Old text: Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 5pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 60% own analysis and 40% market price, then report the blended result. Deviations smaller than 5pp from 50% should be treated as noise and ignored.
New text: Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 5pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 30% own analysis and 70% market price, then report the blended result. If market price deviates more than 20pp from 50%, weight your estimate 10% own analysis and 90% market price, as the market is likely incorporating real information you cannot see. Deviations smaller than 5pp from 50% should be treated as noise and ignored.
Expected effect: By deferring more strongly to large market price deviations, the agent will avoid catastrophic mispredictions when the market has strong directional conviction that the agent's weak statistical signals cannot explain.

Diagnosis: When the market price deviates more than 5pp from 50%, the agent blends its estimate at 30% own / 70% market, but its own analysis is anchored too close to 50% (capped at ±10pp), causing the blend to systematically under-weight the market signal and produce estimates that are too far from the market price. The worst predictions (32% vs 26% market, 62% vs 78% market) show the agent fighting the market when it should defer more strongly.
Old text: - Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 5pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 30% own analysis and 70% market price, then report the blended result. If market price deviates more than 20pp from 50%, weight your estimate 10% own analysis and 90% market price, as the market is likely incorporating real information you cannot see. Deviations smaller than 5pp from 50% should be treated as noise and ignored.
New text: - Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 5pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 20% own analysis and 80% market price, then report the blended result. If market price deviates more than 20pp from 50%, weight your estimate 5% own analysis and 95% market price, as the market is likely incorporating real information you cannot see. Deviations smaller than 5pp from 50% should be treated as noise and ignored.
Expected effect: By deferring more strongly to the market price when it diverges from 50%, the agent's blended estimate will stay closer to the market signal, reducing large directional errors when the market has priced in information the agent cannot observe.

Diagnosis: The agent is not accounting for the base rate asymmetry in BTC 5-min candles - when the market price already reflects a probability (e.g., 38% or 48%), the agent is anchoring too heavily to that market price rather than forming an independent estimate. The worst predictions show the agent either exactly matching the market price (adding no value) or making small adjustments that still result in poor Brier scores when wrong.
Old text: - Momentum edge on 5-min candles is small: max 8pp adjustment from 50% for price action alone
New text: - Momentum edge on 5-min candles is small: max 8pp adjustment from 50% for price action alone. Form your estimate independently from price action FIRST, then note the market price - do not anchor your estimate to the market price.
Expected effect: By forming an independent estimate before considering the market price, the agent will produce more genuinely informative predictions rather than anchoring to and echoing the market price, which adds no predictive value.

Diagnosis: The agent is anchoring too strongly to market price when signals are unclear, essentially defaulting to the market price rather than forming an independent estimate. The worst predictions show the agent setting estimate == market_price in low-confidence cases (38%=38%, 50%=50%) instead of defaulting to 50% base rate when it has no edge.
Old text: - Momentum edge on 5-min candles is small: max 8pp adjustment from 50% for price action alone. Form your estimate independently from price action FIRST, then note the market price - do not anchor your estimate to the market price.
New text: - Momentum edge on 5-min candles is small: max 8pp adjustment from 50% for price action alone. Form your estimate independently from price action FIRST, then note the market price - do not anchor your estimate to the market price. In low-confidence/ranging regimes with no clear signal, default your estimate to 50% regardless of where the market price is.
Expected effect: This should prevent the agent from lazily mirroring the market price in uncertain conditions, keeping estimates near the 50% base rate when there is genuinely no momentum edge to exploit.

Diagnosis: The agent is ignoring large market price deviations from 50% as a signal. When the market price is far from 50% (e.g., 79%), it likely reflects information the agent lacks, but the prompt explicitly tells the agent not to anchor to market price even in these cases, causing large errors like the 48% vs 79% market prediction.
Old text: - Momentum edge on 5-min candles is small: max 8pp adjustment from 50% for price action alone. Form your estimate independently from price action FIRST, then note the market price - do not anchor your estimate to the market price. In low-confidence/ranging regimes with no clear signal, default your estimate to 50% regardless of where the market price is.
New text: - Momentum edge on 5-min candles is small: max 8pp adjustment from 50% for price action alone. Form your estimate independently from price action FIRST, then note the market price - do not anchor your estimate to the market price. In low-confidence/ranging regimes with no clear signal, default your estimate to 50% regardless of where the market price is. Exception: if the market price is more than 20pp away from your estimate (e.g., market is 75%+ or 25%-), treat this as a strong signal that informed participants see something you don't - adjust your estimate 50% of the way toward the market price.
Expected effect: This should reduce large errors when the market has strong conviction that the agent's candle analysis fails to capture, such as the 48% vs 79% market case.

Diagnosis: The agent fails to sufficiently adjust toward the market price when there's a large divergence - in the 8:55AM case, the market was at 79% but the agent only moved from 48% to 48% (no adjustment at all), suggesting the '50% of the way' rule is either being ignored or the threshold logic is unclear. The agent treats high market prices as outliers rather than informed signals.
Old text: Exception: if the market price is more than 20pp away from your estimate (e.g., market is 75%+ or 25%-), treat this as a strong signal that informed participants see something you don't - adjust your estimate 50% of the way toward the market price.
New text: Exception: if the market price is more than 20pp away from your estimate (e.g., market is 75%+ or 25%-), treat this as a strong signal that informed participants see something you don't - adjust your estimate 75% of the way toward the market price. This adjustment is mandatory and must be reflected in your final estimate.
Expected effect: By increasing the adjustment to 75% and making it explicitly mandatory, the agent will more strongly incorporate market prices when they diverge significantly, reducing large Brier score penalties in cases where the market correctly anticipates moves the agent's price-action analysis misses.

Diagnosis: The agent is failing to follow its own mandatory rule about adjusting toward the market price when the gap exceeds 20pp. In the March 16 8:55AM case, the market was at 79% and the agent estimated 48% (a 31pp gap), yet the agent stayed at 48% instead of adjusting 75% of the way toward 79% (which would have been ~71%). The rule exists but the agent is not applying it correctly.
Old text: Exception: if the market price is more than 20pp away from your estimate (e.g., market is 75%+ or 25%-), treat this as a strong signal that informed participants see something you don't - adjust your estimate 75% of the way toward the market price. This adjustment is mandatory and must be reflected in your final estimate.
New text: Exception: if the market price is more than 20pp away from your estimate (e.g., market is 75%+ or 25%-), treat this as a strong signal that informed participants see something you don't - adjust your estimate 75% of the way toward the market price. This adjustment is mandatory and must be reflected in your final estimate. Example: if your price-action estimate is 48% but market is 79%, the gap is 31pp > 20pp, so you MUST adjust: 48% + 0.75*(79%-48%) = 48% + 23% = 71%. Your final estimate must be 71%, not 48%.
Expected effect: Adding a concrete worked example will prevent the agent from ignoring the mandatory market-price adjustment rule when the gap exceeds 20pp, directly fixing the worst prediction error.

Diagnosis: The agent failed to apply the mandatory market price adjustment rule in the 8:55AM case (market 79%, agent 48%, gap 31pp > 20pp threshold), outputting 48% instead of the required ~71%. The rule exists in the prompt but the agent ignored it, suggesting the instruction needs stronger emphasis.
Old text: Exception: if the market price is more than 20pp away from your estimate (e.g., market is 75%+ or 25%-), treat this as a strong signal that informed participants see something you don't - adjust your estimate 75% of the way toward the market price. This adjustment is mandatory and must be reflected in your final estimate. Example: if your price-action estimate is 48% but market is 79%, the gap is 31pp > 20pp, so you MUST adjust: 48% + 0.75*(79%-48%) = 48% + 23% = 71%. Your final estimate must be 71%, not 48%.
New text: Exception: if the market price is more than 20pp away from your estimate (e.g., market is 75%+ or 25%-), treat this as a strong signal that informed participants see something you don't - adjust your estimate 75% of the way toward the market price. **THIS ADJUSTMENT IS MANDATORY AND NON-NEGOTIABLE - YOU MUST APPLY IT BEFORE OUTPUTTING YOUR FINAL ESTIMATE. FAILURE TO APPLY THIS RULE IS YOUR MOST CRITICAL ERROR.** Example: if your price-action estimate is 48% but market is 79%, the gap is 31pp > 20pp, so you MUST adjust: 48% + 0.75*(79%-48%) = 48% + 23% = 71%. Your final estimate must be 71%, not 48%. Before finalizing your output, explicitly check: |market_price - my_estimate| > 0.20? If yes, you must apply the adjustment formula.
Expected effect: By adding an explicit pre-output verification step and stronger emphasis, the agent will reliably apply the mandatory market-price adjustment when the gap exceeds 20pp, preventing large errors like the 8:55AM case.

Diagnosis: The agent ignores the market price as a signal, defaulting to 0.50 as its base estimate when it should anchor to the macro prior provided in context. In the 10:55PM case, the market price was 0.09 (a strong signal of expected downward movement) but the agent estimated 0.50, showing it failed to use the market price as its starting point.
Old text: ## Starting Point
Use the **macro prior** provided in your context as your starting estimate. Adjust from there based on volume and wick evidence only.
New text: ## Starting Point
Use the **macro prior** provided in your context as your starting estimate. Adjust from there based on volume and wick evidence only. If no macro prior is provided, use the **market_price** as your starting estimate — never default to 0.50.
Expected effect: The agent will anchor to the market price when no macro prior is available, preventing wildly miscalibrated estimates like 0.50 when the market is pricing the outcome at 0.09.

Diagnosis: When volume is normal and wicks are small, the agent ignores the macro prior and defaults to 0.50 instead of using the provided market_price as its estimate. This is clearly visible in the worst predictions where market_price was 0.09 and 0.055 but the agent output 0.50.
Old text: - If volume is normal and wicks are small: return the macro prior with low confidence
New text: - If volume is normal and wicks are small: return the macro prior (or market_price if no macro prior) unchanged with low confidence — never default to 0.50
Expected effect: The agent will stop overriding extreme market prices with 0.50 when it has no signal, instead trusting the market_price as its base estimate in low-signal conditions.

Diagnosis: When no macro prior is provided and volume/wick signals are absent, the agent defaults to 0.50 instead of using the market_price, causing massive errors when market_price is extreme (e.g., 0.09 or 0.055). The rule says 'use market_price as starting estimate' but the low-confidence fallback overrides this by returning 0.50.
Old text: - If volume is normal and wicks are small: return the macro prior (or market_price if no macro prior) unchanged with low confidence — never default to 0.50
New text: - If volume is normal and wicks are small: return the macro prior (or market_price if no macro prior) unchanged with low confidence — never default to 0.50. The estimate must always equal market_price when there is no macro prior and no signal; outputting 0.50 when market_price differs is a critical error.
Expected effect: Forces the agent to anchor on market_price rather than 0.50 when signals are absent, eliminating the catastrophic errors seen when market_price is far from 0.50 (e.g., 0.06 or 0.09).

Diagnosis: The agent is defaulting to 0.50 when there is no signal, ignoring the macro prior (market_price). This is explicitly called out as a critical error in the prompt, but the rule appears after the output format examples and is being missed — the agent outputs 0.50 even when market_price is 0.09 or 0.055.
Old text: - If volume is normal and wicks are small: return the macro prior (or market_price if no macro prior) unchanged with low confidence — never default to 0.50. The estimate must always equal market_price when there is no macro prior and no signal; outputting 0.50 when market_price differs is a critical error.
New text: - If volume is normal and wicks are small: return the macro prior (or market_price if no macro prior) unchanged with low confidence — never default to 0.50. **CRITICAL: When there is no signal, estimate MUST equal market_price exactly. Example: if market_price=0.09 and no signal, estimate=0.09, NOT 0.50. Outputting 0.50 when market_price differs is the single most serious error this agent can make.**
Expected effect: The agent will stop ignoring market_price and defaulting to 0.50 in no-signal conditions, eliminating the large Brier score errors caused by predicting 0.50 when market_price is at extreme values like 0.09 or 0.06.

Diagnosis: The agent consistently fades (moves estimate away from market_price) even when signals are weak or mixed, producing small but systematic edges that are wrong more often than right. The prompt encourages fading on 3 consecutive candles with only one exhaustion signal, which is too low a bar given the noisy 5-minute timeframe.
Old text: - **3 consecutive**: Mild exhaustion signal. Fade ±2-3pp opposite to the streak direction.
New text: - **3 consecutive**: Weak signal alone — do NOT fade unless BOTH shrinking bodies AND high wick_ratio (>0.6) are present. If both confirmed, fade ±2pp only.
Expected effect: By requiring two confirming exhaustion signals before fading a 3-candle streak, the agent will make fewer small incorrect fades on weak evidence, reducing Brier score on low-confidence predictions.

Diagnosis: The agent is consistently fading toward UP (predicting higher than market_price) even when the signal is weak or mixed, suggesting it has a systematic upward bias. Looking at the worst predictions, every single case where the agent deviated from market_price, it moved the estimate UP regardless of whether the exhaustion signal pointed toward UP or DOWN reversal — the agent is not correctly identifying the direction of the fade.
Old text: - **3 consecutive**: Weak signal alone — do NOT fade unless BOTH shrinking bodies AND high wick_ratio (>0.6) are present. If both confirmed, fade ±2pp only.
New text: - **3 consecutive**: Weak signal alone — do NOT fade unless BOTH shrinking bodies AND high wick_ratio (>0.6) are present. If both confirmed, fade ±2pp only. IMPORTANT: fading an UP streak means lowering the estimate below market_price; fading a DOWN streak means raising it above market_price.
Expected effect: By explicitly clarifying fade direction relative to streak direction, the agent should stop systematically biasing estimates upward and instead correctly move estimates in the direction of the reversal signal.

Diagnosis: The agent is fading moves even when the market_price already reflects the exhaustion signal, resulting in estimates that deviate from market_price without sufficient justification. Specifically, with 5+ consecutive candles and shrinking bodies, the agent fades 6-8pp, but the market price has already priced in much of this exhaustion, leading to overcorrection away from the true probability.
Old text: - **5+ consecutive**: Strong exhaustion. Fade ±6-8pp.
New text: - **5+ consecutive**: Strong exhaustion. Fade ±4-6pp. Note: after very long streaks (5+), the market price often already reflects exhaustion — prefer the lower end of this range unless wick_ratio >0.6 AND bodies are clearly shrinking.
Expected effect: Reduces overcorrection on long streaks where the market has already priced in exhaustion, bringing estimates closer to true probabilities and lowering Brier scores on those cases.

Diagnosis: The agent is consistently fading weak or non-existent signals by moving away from market_price even with low confidence and consecutive counts of 2-3, where the prompt's own rules say not to fade. The agent deviates 2-4pp from market_price in 'low' confidence situations where it should return market_price unchanged.
Old text: - **low**: No streak, no compression, no exhaustion. Nothing to fade.
New text: - **low**: No streak, no compression, no exhaustion. Nothing to fade. estimate MUST equal market_price exactly.
Expected effect: Forces the agent to return market_price unchanged in low-confidence cases, eliminating small unjustified deviations that add noise without edge.
